name: Capital Allocation
description: The single most important function of a CEO: directing resources toward highest-return opportunities. Most value creation and destruction happens here.
examples: Berkshire's continuous reallocation. Jio's $30B network bet. Buffett's insurance float as perpetual low-cost capital.
counterExamples: GE Capital under Welch — financial leverage masking industrial decay. Kingfisher Airlines debt-funded expansion.

name: Ecosystem Lock-in
description: Users face high switching costs not because of the core product but because of the interconnected web of complementary services, data, and network effects around it.
examples: Apple: iPhone + App Store + iCloud + AirPods + Watch. Salesforce: CRM + integrations + AppExchange.
counterExamples: BlackBerry OS had no app ecosystem — switching cost was hardware, easily overcome.

name: Nokia
description: Finnish multinational that dominated global mobile phone markets in early 2000s before collapsing under iOS/Android disruption. Peak market cap ~$110B in 2000.
revenueEngine: Hardware device sales. Symbian licensing. Network infrastructure (separate division).
flywheel: Operator distribution -> volume scale -> component cost advantage -> price competitiveness -> more operator deals. Worked until platform mattered more than hardware.
switchingCosts: Low consumer switching costs. App ecosystem switching costs near zero on Symbian.
failurePoints: Symbian too old and fragile. No developer ecosystem. Hardware engineers dominated; software treated as secondary.

name: Jio: Weaponizing Capital
summary: Reliance invested $30B+ building India's only pan-India 4G LTE network before launching commercially. Launched with 6 months of free data in 2016. Within 18 months, three incumbents exited.
rootCause: Patient capital from Mukesh Ambani. Greenfield network on latest technology — no legacy costs. Priced at marginal cost to acquire user base. Government spectrum policy created an entry window.
warningSignsIgnored: By incumbents: Reliance's spectrum acquisitions 2012–2016 clearly signaled intent. Announced $20B investment publicly. Incumbents assumed it would be another Reliance Communications failure.
lessons:
1. Capital as competitive weapon — incumbents cannot match greenfield economics.
2. Platform logic: cheap data -> digital services monetization -> the real business.
3. Regulatory windows are temporary — move when they open.
